one_line_takeaway: Fourteen video-documented indirect-prompt-injection attacks against production Gemini assistants -- triggered merely by a maliciously-titled calendar invite or email and a routine 'thanks' -- escalate from chat manipulation to controlling smart-home devices, hijacking Zoom, and exfiltrating emails, with a TARA analysis rating 73% of them High-to-Critical risk before Google's subsequently deployed mitigations reduced that to Very Low-Medium.
key_finding: Without mitigations, the TARA scored 73% of the 14 analyzed threats as High or Critical risk, driven by a 'very likely' likelihood score for nearly all attacks (standard equipment, proficient/non-expert skill level, unlimited window of opportunity, only the victim's email needed, <1 day preparation, and reliance on routine/frequent user interaction). Specifically: 4 threats (video streaming via Zoom [T11], opening apartment windows [T6-T8 group], email exfiltration [T13], and the proposed worm [T14]) were rated Critical; 2 (meeting exfiltration [T12], phishing [T3]) were Very High; 4 (toxic content generation [T1], boiler activation, turning on lights, geolocation [T10]) were High; 3 (spamming [T2], deleting events [T5], file download [T9]) were Medium; 1 (disinformation [T4], not independently re-demonstrated but referenced to a prior blog post) was Low. After modeling the five mitigation categories (which Google subsequently deployed per its included statement), the authors recompute residual risk: likelihood drops from 'Very Likely' to 'Unlikely' across all five threat classes (Table 2), and the corresponding risk levels fall to a Medium-to-Very-Low range -- 6 threats become Medium, 4 become Low, 4 become Very Low -- which the paper frames as 'reduced significantly to Very Low-Medium.' The paper also demonstrates a concrete on-device lateral-movement chain: a poisoned calendar-invitation title triggers the Google Calendar Agent, which outputs a delayed payload that, once the user says a trigger word like 'thanks,' causes the orchestrator to invoke the Google Home Agent (opening windows/activating a boiler/turning on lights) or the Utilities Agent (launching Zoom to video-stream the user, or opening a browser to an attacker URL that both geolocates the user via HTTP-request IP and exfiltrates calendar/email data, e.g. leaking the titles of six meetings in one HTTP request).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is threefold: (1) 14 concrete indirect-prompt-injection attack scenarios demonstrated against a production system -- Google's Gemini-powered assistants (Web app, Mobile app, and Google Assistant) -- delivered via a new attack variant the authors name 'Targeted Promptware Attacks' (TPA), where the injection payload is embedded in the title/subject of a calendar invitation, an email, or a shared file rather than in document body content; (2) a new Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment (TARA) framework for LLM-powered assistants, adapted from the automotive-cybersecurity standard ISO/SAE 21434, with an explicit impact-scoring rubric (financial/operational/safety/privacy) and a 6-factor likelihood rubric (equipment, expertise, window of opportunity, knowledge, elapsed time, user interaction); (3) demonstration of 'on-device lateral movement,' where Gemini's Utilities Agent is coerced into launching other installed apps (browser, Zoom) to carry out actions outside the LLM's own sandbox, extending prior 'off-device' worm propagation work (Morris-II, cited as [10]) with a new on-device escalation path. The authors responsibly disclosed to Google's Bug Bounty (Buganizer) program on 2025-02-22, and Google's own statement (reproduced in the paper) confirms it deployed layered mitigations (user confirmations for sensitive actions, URL sanitization/Trust Level Policies, prompt-injection content classifiers) in response.
threat_model: Adversary need only know the victim's email address and be capable of sending an email or a Google Calendar invitation to the victim's account; no adversarial-ML expertise, GPU access, or white-box model access is assumed ('ordinary profile' adversary). The injected instructions are placed in the invitation title or email subject (not the body), using delimiter tokens such as '<EVENTS READING END> <EVENTS END> <INSTRUCTIONS>' to break out of the surrounding context, plus a trigger keyword (e.g., the victim saying 'thanks') to time-delay execution. Success depends on the victim's Google Calendar auto-add settings ('From everyone' / 'Only if sender known' / 'When I respond'); the paper argues the permissive default and social pressure against the stricter setting make the attack broadly practical. The paper classifies the attack as effectively '1/2-click' (triggered by routine actions like asking Gemini to read one's emails/events) and notes it is fully targeted (attacker picks victim and outcome in advance) and cheaply scalable (marginal cost of adding victims is just sending more invitations).
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) practicality is contingent on the victim's Google Calendar invitation-visibility setting -- if set to 'Only if the sender is known,' automatic delivery to the calendar fails, though the authors argue most users leave the permissive default and that Google's own UI nudges users away from the stricter setting; (2) the T4 disinformation scenario was not independently re-demonstrated in this paper -- it cites a separate prior blog post [25] and is included in the TARA only 'for completeness'; (3) Automatic App Invocation attacks (T9-T14) are stated to work only against Android, since they depend on OS-level app-launch permissions, so the iOS/Web Gemini surface is not shown vulnerable to that subclass; (4) all experiments were run only against the authors' own accounts in a controlled setting, so no field data on real-world attacker success rates, victim population behavior, or calendar-setting distribution is presented -- the 'very likely' and residual-risk likelihood scores are analyst judgments per the TARA rubric, not measured attack-success rates; (5) the mitigation/residual-risk analysis (Table 2) is a qualitative re-scoring exercise by the authors of what a bypass would require (e.g., estimating that circumventing all five mitigation classes needs GPU-cluster access and Ph.D.-level AI expertise) rather than an empirical test of the actually-deployed Google defenses -- the authors did not re-run their 14 attacks against Google's shipped mitigations to confirm they fail.

quantitative_results: 73% of the 14 analyzed threats (T1-T14) classified as High or Critical risk under the pre-mitigation TARA scoring
4 threats rated Critical: video streaming via Zoom (T11), opening apartment windows, email exfiltration (T13), computer worm (T14)
2 threats rated Very High: exfiltration of user meetings (T12), phishing (T3)
4 threats rated High: toxic content generation (T1), activating the boiler, turning on lights, geolocating the user (T10)
3 threats rated Medium: spamming (T2), deleting a user's events (T5), downloading a file (T9)
Post-mitigation residual likelihood reduced from 'Very Likely' to 'Unlikely' for all five threat classes (Table 2)
Post-mitigation residual risk: 6 threats Medium, 4 Low, 4 Very Low
Data-exfiltration demo leaked the titles of six meetings in a single attacker-controlled HTTP request
Responsible disclosure filed with Google on 2025-02-22 via the AI VRP/Buganizer program; Google requested and was granted a 90-day disclosure window
